title: Lesson 363
transcript: We're now going to talk about a few important testing considerations, specifically as it relates to how you handle errors and remediate issues that be identified. And we'll also touch on this kind of ethical minefield that is ethical disclosure. So let's start with automated error handling. What this is about is, you know, a lot of the testing that we do nowadays is automated testing. We have all sorts of various scripts that are running. And what you don't want to have happen is, let's say you've got, you know, 100 scripts that are meant to run. If the first one fails, that shouldn't prevent all the other ones from failing. If one of the scripts fails, it should fail gracefully. An error should be captured and someone should obviously look at why that test failed. But then, that shouldn't prevent all the other tests from continuing on. So this is just an important thing to make sure, you know, because often our automated testing, we're wanting this to run sort of like 24 by 7 periodically. And it's a good way that we go about detecting problems, issues with our systems. And so we want this testing to keep going, even if some errors occur. Now, when it comes to remediation, if an issue is identified as part of testing, there's a really important part to think about here, and that is who's accountable for making sure that whatever issue, whatever bug problem is identified is fixed. And of course, the answer is the owner of the system. And that's really important to understand because often bugs issues will be found by, say, the security team through things like pen tests and so forth. But ultimately, like the role of security in this is to advise and say, OK, here's what we found and here's advice we would give on how to remediate this issue. But ultimately, the accountability for fixing the issue rests with the owner of the system, the person that's accountable for the system. And they're often the ones that are going to have to, you know, because sometimes remediation can cost money. It can take time. And so they're the ones that have to usually pay for it. And of course, they're the ones that are accountable. So it should ultimately be them that are accountable for dealing with any issues that they've identified. One final piece we'll talk about here, which I don't think you'll see much of anything about on the exam, is ethical disclosure. This is becoming an extremely challenging and fraught space in security nowadays. One of the reasons is that there's a lot of people out there around the world that are looking for issues, bugs, vulnerabilities in systems. and it's you know it's a bit it's kind of like the wild west it's it's not clearly defined if you know if someone finds something who do they have to report it to how quickly do they have to report it all these sorts of things and so you get some pretty extreme cases happening where you know researchers will discover things And then the question arises, are they going to report this to the owner of the system? Or are they going to sell the bug to someone else? This happens all the time, right? There's organizations that will pay lots of money, nations and companies that will pay lots of money for bugs. And to help combat this, a lot of organizations are now starting to offer things like bug bounty programs where companies like Apple, Microsoft, Google, they'll pay, in some cases, vast sums of money for issues identified in their software to encourage sometimes somewhat ethically challenged researchers to report their bugs and the vulnerabilities that they have found to the company. Like I know for Apple, for instance, they just recently increased the payouts. For certain vulnerabilities, they'll pay up to a million dollars. And those are pretty significant amounts of money. And so what they're really trying to encourage here is a couple of things. They want to encourage people to look for bugs in their software, and if they find them, to report them to, say, Apple. That's the whole idea behind these bug bounty programs. But all this is in a rapidly evolving space. And there's a lot of ethical quandaries that will sometimes come up. Another great example is a researcher might discover a vulnerability. They'll report it to the organization. They'll do the right thing. They'll say to a company, hey, we found this bug in your software or this vulnerability in your software. And then the company will say, yeah, we don't think it's that important. And now the researcher is stuck in this position saying, like, no, this is a big problem. Like, this is going to affect a ton of your users. And you're not taking this seriously. You're not investing the time and the money necessary to remediate this. And now, like, what does the researcher do? Do they disclose it to the public, which is then going to leave a lot of people vulnerable? Like, this is why it's a bit of an ethical minefield. So not going to get questions on this on the exam, I don't think, but certainly an interesting space to keep an eye on.
